Albania.notes: Employment income over ~200k ALL/month is taxed at 23%; capital gains at a flat 15%.

Argentina.notes: High VAT (21%) plus progressive PIT to 35%; corporate tax is tiered up to 35%.

Armenia.specialRegime: 1% IT/microbusiness turnover regimes available for small businesses
Armenia.notes: Flat 20% personal income tax with no brackets or allowances; residents taxed on worldwide income.

Australia.notes: Top 45% rate plus 2% Medicare levy; 50% CGT discount on assets held over 12 months.

Austria.notes: Steep progressive PIT (55% top) plus social security; investment income taxed at flat 27.5%.

Azerbaijan.specialRegime: 0% PIT for private-sector non-oil/gas wages up to 8,000 AZN/month (temporary incentive)
Azerbaijan.notes: Non-oil private-sector salaries enjoy a 0% rate up to 8,000 AZN/month, then 14%.

Bahrain.notes: No personal income tax; a 15% domestic minimum top-up tax applies only to large multinationals.

Barbados.specialRegime: Welcome Stamp 12-month remote-worker visa (foreign income not taxed locally)
Barbados.notes: No capital gains tax and a low 9% corporate rate; PIT tops out at 28.5%.

Belgium.specialRegime: Special tax regime for inbound taxpayers/researchers (expat allowance)
Belgium.notes: Among the world's highest effective tax burdens once social security and 50% top rate apply.

Bolivia.notes: Source-based system: only Bolivian-source income is taxed, via a flat 13% RC-IVA largely offset by VAT credits.

Bosnia and Herzegovina.notes: Flat 10% personal and corporate tax, though high mandatory social contributions add to the wage burden.

Brazil.notes: PIT tops at 27.5%; corporate burden is effectively ~34% and indirect taxes are being reformed into a new VAT.

Bulgaria.notes: EU's lowest flat rate: 10% on both personal and corporate income.

Cambodia.notes: Salary tax tops at 20% for residents; non-residents pay a flat 20% final tax.

Canada.notes: Combined federal-plus-provincial top rates exceed 50%; capital gains are 50% included in income.

Chile.specialRegime: New residents taxed only on Chilean-source income for first 3 years
Chile.notes: Residents are taxed on worldwide income, but newcomers get a 3-year foreign-income exemption before full worldwide taxation begins.

China.specialRegime: Six-year rule can shield foreign-source income for non-domiciled residents
China.notes: Non-domiciled residents can avoid worldwide taxation if they break the 6-year residence streak with a 30+ day absence.

Colombia.notes: Residents are taxed on worldwide income with a high 35% corporate rate and one of Latin America's heavier overall tax loads.

Costa Rica.specialRegime: Territorial system; foreign-source income generally untaxed
Costa Rica.notes: Only Costa Rican-source income is taxed, making foreign salaries, pensions and investment income generally exempt for residents.

Croatia.specialRegime: Digital nomad visa with tax exemption on foreign income during stay
Croatia.notes: Croatia's digital nomad residence permit exempts qualifying foreign-earned income, though standard residents face worldwide taxation.

Cuba.notes: Cuba's state-dominated tax system carries steep marginal rates and a closed currency/banking environment that complicates any relocation.

Curaçao.specialRegime: Penshonado regime: reduced ~10% rate on foreign income for qualifying retirees
Curaçao.notes: Standard rates are high, but the 'penshonado' regime offers retirees a flat reduced rate on foreign-source income.

Czech Republic.specialRegime: Flat-rate lump-sum tax scheme for self-employed (paušální daň)
Czech Republic.notes: A flat 15% rate (23% above ~CZK 1.76M) plus mandatory social/health contributions push the effective burden for high earners well above the headline.

Denmark.specialRegime: Researcher/expat scheme: ~32.84% flat for up to 7 years
Denmark.notes: Among the world's highest personal taxes, but qualifying expats and researchers can use a ~33% flat-rate scheme for up to 7 years.

Dominican Republic.specialRegime: Foreign-source income exempt for first 3 years of residency
Dominican Republic.notes: Largely territorial: foreign income is exempt for new residents' first three years and foreign investment income is taxed only loosely thereafter.

Ecuador.notes: Residents are taxed on worldwide income; the dollarized economy simplifies banking but the top marginal rate reaches 37%.

Egypt.specialRegime: Foreign-source income of residents generally not taxed unless Egypt is the center of commercial activity
Egypt.notes: Egypt largely taxes only locally-sourced income, so foreign salaries and investments are typically outside the net for residents.

Estonia.specialRegime: Flat tax; corporate tax deferred until profits are distributed
Estonia.notes: A flat 22% personal rate (24% VAT from 2025) and a distribution-only corporate tax make Estonia attractive for entrepreneurs reinvesting profits.

Ethiopia.notes: Tax applies mainly to Ethiopian-source income, but currency controls and limited financial infrastructure are the bigger practical hurdles.

Fiji.specialRegime: Foreign-source income of residents generally exempt; low flat top rate
Fiji.notes: Fiji caps personal income tax at 20% and largely exempts foreign-source income, though a social responsibility levy applies to very high earners.

Finland.specialRegime: Key-employee flat 32% tax for inbound foreign experts (limited period)
Finland.notes: High combined state, municipal and social tax burden; capital gains taxed at 30/34%.

France.specialRegime: Impatriate regime: partial exemption of bonus/foreign income up to 8 years
France.notes: High social charges plus a 30% flat tax (PFU) on investment income and capital gains.

French Polynesia.specialRegime: No personal income tax; only a progressive CST social contribution 0.5-5%
French Polynesia.notes: No income tax on individuals, just a small solidarity contribution (CST) on wages.

Georgia.specialRegime: 1% small-business / individual-entrepreneur regime; foreign-source income exempt
Georgia.notes: Flat 20% wage tax but foreign-sourced income is generally untaxed and a 1% IE regime exists.

Germany.notes: Top 42% marginal hits at ~EUR 68k; solidarity surcharge and church tax may add on top.

Ghana.notes: Progressive PIT up to 35%; effective VAT exceeds 15% once levies (NHIL/GETFund/COVID) are added.

Greece.specialRegime: EUR 100k flat tax for new HNW residents; 50% exemption for relocating professionals; 7% on foreign pensions
Greece.notes: Several expat regimes (lump-sum, 7% pensioner, 50% work exemption) sharply cut the headline 44% rate.

Guatemala.specialRegime: Territorial system; only Guatemalan-source income is taxed
Guatemala.notes: Very low personal tax (5-7%) and a territorial system exempting foreign income.

Honduras.specialRegime: Territorial system; foreign-source income generally not taxed
Honduras.notes: Progressive PIT up to 25%; corporate income can carry a 5% solidarity surcharge above thresholds.

Hungary.specialRegime: Flat 15% PIT and lowest EU corporate rate (9%)
Hungary.notes: Flat 15% income tax and 9% corporate tax, but the highest VAT in the world at 27%.

Iceland.specialRegime: 75% of income exempt for 3 years for qualifying foreign experts
Iceland.notes: Three-band system reaching ~46%; flat 22% capital gains and municipal tax embedded.

India.specialRegime: New optional regime with lower rates; RNOR status shields foreign income for new returnees
India.notes: Top effective rate ~39% with surcharge; long-term capital gains taxed at 12.5%, GST standard 18%.

Indonesia.specialRegime: Qualifying foreign expats taxed only on Indonesian-source income for first 4 years
Indonesia.notes: Progressive PIT up to 35%; new expat workers can get a 4-year territorial-style break.

Ireland.specialRegime: Non-dom remittance basis; SARP relief reduces tax on high earners' income
Ireland.notes: 40% rate plus USC and PRSI push the effective marginal over 50%; non-doms taxed only on remitted foreign income.

Israel.specialRegime: New/returning residents: 10-year exemption on foreign income and gains
Israel.notes: Top rate ~50% with surtax, but new immigrants get a decade-long exemption on foreign-source income.

Italy.specialRegime: Impatriate regime (50% income exemption) and €200k flat tax on foreign income for new high-net-worth residents
Italy.notes: Worldwide taxation with high IRPEF plus regional/municipal surtaxes, but generous new-resident regimes can sharply cut the effective rate.

Japan.specialRegime: Non-permanent residents taxed only on Japan-source and remitted foreign income for first 5 years
Japan.notes: National income tax up to 45% plus a flat 10% local inhabitant tax, so effective rates climb fast at higher incomes.

Jordan.notes: Largely territorial system taxing Jordan-source income, with a 1% national contribution added on top of the income-tax brackets.

Kazakhstan.notes: Flat 10% personal income tax makes it very low-tax for professionals, though residents are taxed on worldwide income.

Kenya.notes: Progressive PAYE reaching 35%, and employment income earned abroad by residents is also taxable.

Kuwait.notes: No personal income tax and no VAT; 15% corporate tax applies only to foreign-owned companies, with a new 15% top-up tax on large multinationals from 2025.

Laos.notes: Progressive personal income tax topping out at 25%, with a low effective capital-gains/transfer tax on share sales.

Latvia.specialRegime: Corporate tax deferred until profits are distributed (0% on retained earnings)
Latvia.notes: Progressive PIT up to 33% plus mandatory social contributions, but corporate tax is only due when profits are distributed.

Lebanon.notes: Schedular, largely territorial tax system taxing Lebanon-source income, but currency and banking instability are the real concerns for movers.

Lithuania.notes: Employment income taxed at 20%/32% with a 32% rate above roughly 60 average wages, plus social contributions on top.

Malaysia.specialRegime: Foreign-sourced income of resident individuals exempt (subject to conditions) through 2036
Malaysia.notes: Territorial in practice for individuals, with foreign income remitted to Malaysia exempt until 2036 and no general capital-gains tax on most assets.

Malta.specialRegime: Non-dom remittance basis; foreign income not remitted is untaxed (annual minimum tax may apply)
Malta.notes: Non-domiciled residents are taxed only on Malta-source income and foreign income they remit, making it attractive for expats despite a 35% headline rate.

Mauritius.specialRegime: Foreign income taxed only when remitted; no capital-gains tax
Mauritius.notes: Low flat-ish rates (top band 20% from July 2025), no capital-gains tax, and foreign income taxed only on remittance.

Mexico.notes: Residents are taxed on worldwide income with a 35% top bracket, and capital gains on listed shares are taxed at a flat 10%.

Montenegro.notes: Low headline income-tax bands (9%/15%) but mandatory social contributions push the effective burden on a well-paid professional higher.

Morocco.notes: Progressive PIT tops out at 37%; worldwide income taxed for residents.

Namibia.specialRegime: Source-based system: only Namibian-source income taxed
Namibia.notes: Source-based taxation means foreign income is generally untaxed, and there is no separate capital gains tax.

Nepal.notes: Top marginal reaches 36-39% including surcharge on very high incomes; residents taxed on worldwide income.

Netherlands.specialRegime: 30% ruling (reducing to 27% from 2027) for skilled migrants
Netherlands.notes: Box 3 taxes investments on a deemed-return basis (~36%) rather than realized gains; the 30% ruling materially lowers expat tax.

New Zealand.specialRegime: 4-year transitional-resident exemption on most foreign income
New Zealand.notes: No general capital gains tax, and new migrants get a ~4-year exemption on foreign income.

Nigeria.notes: The 2025 Tax Act (effective Jan 2026) caps the top personal rate at 25% and aligns capital gains with income/corporate rates.

Norway.notes: Worldwide taxation plus a net wealth tax, and an exit tax on unrealized gains when leaving.

Oman.specialRegime: No personal income tax (5% PIT on high earners legislated from 2028)
Oman.notes: Currently no personal income tax, though a 5% tax on incomes above ~OMR 42k is set to start in 2028.

Panama.notes: Pure territorial system means only Panama-source income is taxed, making foreign earnings tax-free.

Paraguay.specialRegime: Territorial 10% flat tax; foreign income exempt
Paraguay.notes: Flat 10% across personal, corporate and VAT, with a territorial system exempting foreign-source income.

Peru.specialRegime: New residents taxed only on Peruvian-source income for first years
Peru.notes: Residents are taxed on worldwide income, but foreign nationals' foreign income is exempt during initial residency.

Philippines.specialRegime: Resident foreigners taxed only on Philippine-source income
Philippines.notes: Resident aliens are taxed only on local-source income, so foreign earnings are generally untaxed.

Poland.specialRegime: Lump-sum tax on foreign income for new residents (PLN 200k/year)
Poland.notes: Top rate is 32% plus a 4% solidarity levy on high incomes; a foreign-income lump-sum regime exists for new residents.

Portugal.specialRegime: IFICI (NHR 2.0): 20% flat on qualifying income for new residents
Portugal.notes: The old NHR closed to new entrants in 2025; its replacement IFICI offers a 20% rate but only for qualifying high-skill activities.

Puerto Rico.specialRegime: Act 60: 4% business income, 0% capital gains/dividends for qualifying residents
Puerto Rico.notes: Under Act 60, bona fide new residents can pay 0% on Puerto Rico-source capital gains and 4% on export-services income.

Qatar.specialRegime: No personal income tax on salaries
Qatar.notes: No personal income tax for individuals; corporate tax of 10% applies mainly to foreign-owned business profits.

Romania.notes: Flat 10% income tax is low, but mandatory social contributions (~35% employee CAS+CASS) push the effective take well above the headline rate; VAT rose to 21% in Aug 2025.

Rwanda.notes: Top 30% PIT rate kicks in at a low threshold, so a well-paid professional effectively pays close to the top rate.

Saudi Arabia.specialRegime: No personal income tax on employment income
Saudi Arabia.notes: No personal income tax on salaries; foreign-owned companies pay 20% corporate tax while Saudi/GCC owners pay 2.5% zakat instead.

Senegal.notes: Progressive PIT tops out at 40% plus a complementary levy, so high earners face a heavy effective burden.

Serbia.notes: Salary tax is a flat 10% but high mandatory social contributions raise the effective rate; annual income tax adds a surtax for top earners.

Seychelles.specialRegime: Territorial system; no tax on foreign-source income
Seychelles.notes: Only Seychelles-source income is taxed and there is no capital gains tax, making foreign income effectively tax-free for residents.

Singapore.specialRegime: Foreign income not remitted is exempt; no capital gains tax
Singapore.notes: No capital gains tax and largely territorial treatment of foreign income make it very tax-efficient for high earners.

Slovakia.notes: PIT is 19%/25% but social and health contributions add substantially to the effective burden; VAT rose to 23% in 2025.

Slovenia.notes: Highly progressive PIT reaching 50% plus 22% employee social contributions makes it a high-tax destination for professionals.

South Africa.notes: Residents are taxed on worldwide income with a 45% top rate; effective CGT inclusion gives roughly an 18% rate on gains.

South Korea.specialRegime: 19% flat-rate election for qualifying foreign workers
South Korea.notes: Foreign workers can elect a 19% flat tax (plus local surtax) for up to 20 years, often beating the progressive scale.

Spain.specialRegime: Beckham Law: 24% flat on Spanish income for new arrivals (up to 6 years)
Spain.notes: The Beckham regime lets qualifying new residents pay a 24% flat rate on income up to EUR 600k and exempts most foreign income for six years.

Sri Lanka.notes: Recent reforms raised PIT to a 36% top rate and VAT to 18%, including 18% VAT on digital services from April 2025.

Sweden.specialRegime: Expert tax relief: 25% of salary exempt for qualifying foreign key personnel
Sweden.notes: Combined municipal and state income tax reaches ~52% for high earners, though an expert-tax relief exempts a quarter of salary for qualifying inbound specialists.

Switzerland.specialRegime: Lump-sum (forfait) taxation for wealthy non-working foreigners; varies by canton
Switzerland.notes: Private capital gains on movable assets are tax-free, but total income-tax burden varies hugely by canton and commune.

Taiwan.specialRegime: Foreign-source income exempt from regular tax but caught by 20% AMT above a threshold
Taiwan.notes: Securities capital gains are largely exempt from income tax and offshore income is only taxed via the alternative minimum tax.

Tanzania.notes: Residents are taxed on worldwide income with the top 30% rate reached at a low income level.

Thailand.specialRegime: LTR visa with foreign-income exemption; remittance rule eased for income brought in within the year earned or the next
Thailand.notes: Since 2024 foreign income remitted to Thailand is taxable, though a proposed rule exempts it if brought in the year earned or the following year.

UAE.specialRegime: 0% personal income tax; 9% corporate tax only on business profit above AED 375k
UAE.notes: No personal income or capital gains tax, but a 9% corporate tax now applies to business profits over AED 375,000.

United Kingdom.specialRegime: Old non-dom remittance basis abolished from April 2025; replaced by a 4-year FIG regime for new arrivals
United Kingdom.notes: The non-dom regime ended in April 2025 and was replaced by a 4-year exemption on foreign income and gains for new UK residents.

United States.notes: The US taxes citizens and green-card holders on worldwide income regardless of where they live, with no escape via relocation alone.

Uruguay.specialRegime: 11-year tax holiday on foreign investment income for new residents, or a 12% flat rate election
Uruguay.notes: New residents can elect an 11-year exemption (or 12% flat rate) on foreign investment income, but the easy 60-day route to residency was removed for 2026.

Vietnam.notes: Residents are taxed on worldwide income with steep progressive rates reaching 35%, while securities transfers are taxed at 0.1% of sale proceeds.
